title: Example: inference-runtime release changes default attention backend
summary: This labelled example shows how DRIFT presents an inference-runtime release that alters kernel selection and warrants a deployment review.
why_it_matters: An inference-runtime upgrade can change kernel selection, memory use, or supported hardware. Treat this as an example workflow, not a claim about a specific shipped version.
what_to_check: Compare the release notes with your pinned runtime, GPU architecture, serving flags, and latency baseline before promoting the upgrade.

claims.text: The default attention backend is now selected automatically from GPU capability, and a flag is added to pin it.

evidence.excerpt: Default attention backend is now chosen automatically based on GPU capability; pass --attention-backend to override the selection.

claims.text: Automatic backend selection can change latency and memory profiles for operators who previously relied on the implicit default.
Re-run your latency and throughput baseline with the attention backend pinned before promoting the upgrade.

title: Example: framework release surfaces compiler and extension compatibility
summary: This labelled example shows a compatibility-oriented briefing item for a framework release that touches the compiler path.
why_it_matters: Framework releases can expose compiler or extension incompatibilities that only appear in a production build or a less common model path.
what_to_check: Run the affected training or inference smoke test with your pinned compiler and custom extensions before changing the production image.

claims.text: The release raises the minimum supported compiler toolchain used to build custom extensions.

evidence.excerpt: Custom C++/CUDA extensions now require the updated minimum compiler toolchain to build against this release.

claims.text: Rebuild your custom C++/CUDA extensions against this release with your pinned compiler before updating the production image.

evidence.excerpt: Extensions built against the previous toolchain should be recompiled.

title: Example: distributed-communication update affects multi-node collectives
summary: This labelled example shows a lower-priority item, with an explicit confidence signal, for a distributed-communication release.
why_it_matters: Distributed-communication changes can affect multi-node behavior without altering single-node development results.
what_to_check: If you use multi-node training, run a representative collective-communication test before scheduling an upgrade.

claims.text: The release adjusts the default algorithm used for a common collective operation on multi-node topologies.

evidence.excerpt: The default all-reduce algorithm selection has changed for multi-node configurations in this release.

claims.text: Run a representative multi-node collective-communication test before scheduling the upgrade.
